title: What is Next.js and what problems does it solve?
description: The React framework for full-stack apps: rendering, routing, and optimization out of the box.
answer: Next.js is a React framework that adds server-side rendering, static generation, file-based routing, data fetching, bundling, and image/font optimization on top of React, so you ship fast, SEO-friendly full-stack apps without wiring all that infrastructure yourself.

title: What is the difference between the App Router and the Pages Router?
description: Next.js's two routing systems and why the App Router is the modern default.
answer: The Pages Router (pages/) uses getServerSideProps/getStaticProps and client components only. The App Router (app/) is built on React Server Components, with nested layouts, streaming, Server Actions, and granular caching. The App Router is the recommended approach for new apps.

title: What are the rendering strategies in Next.js (SSR, SSG, ISR, CSR)?
description: Server rendering, static generation, incremental regeneration, and client rendering.
answer: SSG pre-renders HTML at build time; SSR renders per request on the server; ISR regenerates static pages in the background on a schedule or on demand; CSR renders in the browser. The App Router chooses static or dynamic per route based on the data/APIs you use.

title: What is the difference between Server Components and Client Components in Next.js?
description: RSC default vs interactive client components.
answer: Server Components render on the server, can access the backend directly, and ship no JS to the browser — they're the default in the App Router. Client Components ('use client') run in the browser and enable state, effects, and event handlers. You compose them, keeping interactivity at the leaves.

title: What is the 'use client' directive and when do you need it?
description: Opting a module (and its imports) into the client bundle.
answer: 'use client' at the top of a file marks it (and the modules it imports) as Client Components, enabling hooks, state, effects, and browser APIs. You need it whenever a component uses interactivity; keep it at the leaves so most of the tree stays server-rendered.

title: How does file-based routing work in the Next.js App Router?
description: Folders map to URL segments; page.js makes a route public.
answer: In the App Router, folders under app/ define URL segments and a page.js (or .tsx) makes that segment a publicly routable page. Special files (layout, loading, error, route) add behavior, and folder names like [id] create dynamic segments.

title: What are the special files in the Next.js App Router (page, layout, loading, error)?
description: Convention files that define UI and behavior per route segment.
answer: page.js (route UI), layout.js (shared shell), loading.js (Suspense fallback), error.js (error boundary), not-found.js (404), route.js (API handler), and template.js (re-mounting layout). Next.js wires them into the route tree automatically.

title: What is hydration in Next.js and what causes a hydration error?
description: Attaching React to server HTML and why mismatches break it.
answer: Hydration is when React attaches event handlers to the server-rendered HTML on the client. A hydration error happens when the client render differs from the server HTML — e.g. using Date.now(), random values, or browser-only APIs during render — so the trees don't match.

title: What are layouts and nested layouts in the Next.js App Router?
description: Shared UI that persists across navigations and nests by folder.
answer: A layout.js wraps its segment and all child segments with shared UI (nav, sidebar) and persists state across navigation without re-rendering. Layouts nest by folder hierarchy, so each level adds its own shell around the children.

title: What is the difference between layout.js and template.js in Next.js?
description: Persistent shared UI vs a fresh instance per navigation.
answer: A layout persists across navigations and keeps state/DOM. A template re-mounts a new instance on every navigation, resetting state and re-running effects — useful for enter/exit animations or per-navigation logic.

title: What are dynamic routes and catch-all segments in Next.js?
description: [id], [...slug], and [[...slug]] route patterns.
answer: A [id] folder creates a dynamic segment whose value is passed via params; [...slug] is a catch-all that matches multiple segments; [[...slug]] is an optional catch-all that also matches the base path. They let one route handle many URLs.

title: What are route groups in Next.js and how do you use them?
description: Folders in parentheses that organize routes without affecting the URL.
answer: A folder named in parentheses, like (marketing), groups routes for organization or to apply a shared layout without adding a URL segment. It lets you have multiple root layouts or separate sections that don't change the path.

title: What are parallel routes (@slot) in Next.js and when would you use them?
description: Rendering multiple independent pages in the same layout simultaneously.
answer: Parallel routes use @folder 'slots' to render multiple pages in the same layout at once (e.g. a feed and a sidebar), each with independent loading/error states. They're ideal for dashboards, modals, and split views.

title: What are intercepting routes in Next.js and what are they used for?
description: Showing a route's content in the current layout (e.g. modals).
answer: Intercepting routes (the (.) / (..) conventions) let you load a route within the current page's context instead of a full navigation — classically to show a photo/detail in a modal while keeping the URL shareable, falling back to the full page on reload.

title: What is loading.js in Next.js and how does Suspense streaming work?
description: Instant loading UI via automatic Suspense boundaries.
answer: Adding loading.js wraps the segment in a Suspense boundary, so Next.js streams an instant fallback while the server component fetches data, then streams in the real content. This improves perceived performance without manual Suspense wiring.

title: What is error.js in Next.js and how does error handling work in the App Router?
description: Segment-level error boundaries with reset.
answer: error.js defines a Client Component error boundary for its segment, catching render errors and showing fallback UI with a reset() to retry. global-error.js catches root-layout errors. Expected errors are handled in code; error.js is for unexpected ones.

title: What is not-found.js in Next.js and how do you trigger a 404?
description: Rendering 404 UI and the notFound() helper.
answer: not-found.js renders the 404 UI for a segment, and calling the notFound() function from a Server Component (e.g. when a record is missing) renders the nearest not-found boundary and returns a 404 status.

title: How does client-side navigation work in Next.js (next/link and useRouter)?
description: SPA-like transitions with prefetching.
answer: next/link renders an anchor that does client-side navigation and prefetches the target route's code/data when it enters the viewport. useRouter()'s push/replace navigate programmatically. Both avoid full page reloads while keeping shared layouts mounted.

title: What are React Server Components and why does Next.js use them?
description: Server-only rendering that ships zero JS for that component.
answer: React Server Components render only on the server, can directly access databases/secrets, and send serialized output (not JS) to the client — reducing bundle size and enabling secure data access. Next.js makes them the default to improve performance and DX.

title: How do Server and Client Components compose in Next.js?
description: Import rules and the children pattern.
answer: A Server Component can render a Client Component, but a Client Component cannot import a Server Component directly — instead you pass Server Components as children/props into Client Components. Props crossing the boundary must be serializable.

title: How do you pass data from Server Components to Client Components in Next.js?
description: Serializable props across the RSC boundary.
answer: You fetch data in a Server Component and pass it as props to a Client Component. The props must be serializable (no functions, classes, or Dates beyond what React can serialize), since they cross the server-to-client boundary in the RSC payload.

title: What is streaming SSR with Suspense in Next.js?
description: Sending HTML in chunks as data resolves.
answer: Streaming SSR sends the shell immediately and streams in parts of the page as their data resolves, using Suspense boundaries (loading.js or <Suspense>). The user sees content faster and slow data doesn't block the whole page.

title: What is Partial Prerendering (PPR) in Next.js?
description: Combining a static shell with streamed dynamic holes.
answer: Partial Prerendering serves a static prerendered shell instantly and streams in the dynamic parts (wrapped in Suspense) within the same response — getting the speed of static with the freshness of dynamic in one route.

title: What is the difference between static and dynamic rendering in the Next.js App Router?
description: Build-time HTML vs per-request rendering.
answer: Static rendering produces HTML at build time (or revalidation) and serves it from cache; dynamic rendering runs per request. A route becomes dynamic when it uses dynamic functions (cookies, headers, searchParams) or uncached data; otherwise Next.js renders it statically.

title: What makes a route dynamic in the Next.js App Router?
description: Dynamic functions and uncached data opt a route out of static rendering.
answer: Using cookies(), headers(), draftMode(), or the searchParams prop, or fetching with no-store / no cache, makes a route dynamic (rendered per request). Without those, Next.js prerenders the route statically by default.

title: What is generateStaticParams in Next.js and how does it enable SSG for dynamic routes?
description: Pre-generating dynamic route pages at build time.
answer: generateStaticParams returns the list of param values (e.g. all blog slugs) so Next.js statically pre-renders those dynamic-route pages at build time. Params not listed can be generated on-demand and cached, depending on dynamicParams config.

title: What is the route segment config in Next.js (dynamic, revalidate, runtime)?
description: Per-segment exports that control rendering and caching.
answer: You export consts from a layout/page/route to control behavior: dynamic ('auto'|'force-dynamic'|'force-static'), revalidate (ISR interval), runtime ('nodejs'|'edge'), fetchCache, and dynamicParams. They override the defaults for that segment.

title: How does Next.js decide between static and dynamic rendering at build time?
description: Automatic detection based on APIs and data usage.
answer: At build, Next.js analyzes each route: if it uses no dynamic functions and only cached data, it's prerendered statically; if it uses cookies/headers/searchParams or uncached fetches, it's marked dynamic. Segment config can force either mode.

title: How do you fetch data in Next.js Server Components?
description: async components with fetch or direct DB access.
answer: Server Components can be async and await data directly — call fetch() (which integrates with Next.js caching) or query your database/ORM straight in the component. No getServerSideProps needed; the data never ships to the client.

title: What are the caching layers in Next.js (Request Memoization, Data Cache, Full Route Cache, Router Cache)?
description: The four overlapping caches in the App Router.
answer: Request Memoization dedupes identical fetches within one render; the Data Cache persists fetch results across requests/deploys; the Full Route Cache stores rendered static routes; the Router Cache holds visited routes client-side for instant back/forward. Each has its own invalidation.

title: How does fetch caching work in the Next.js App Router (force-cache, no-store, revalidate)?
description: Controlling the Data Cache per fetch call.
answer: fetch options control caching: { cache: 'force-cache' } caches indefinitely, { cache: 'no-store' } never caches (dynamic), and { next: { revalidate: N } } caches but revalidates after N seconds (ISR). next: { tags } enables on-demand invalidation.

title: What is ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) in Next.js and how do you implement it?
description: Updating static pages without a full rebuild.
answer: ISR serves a static page and regenerates it in the background after a revalidate interval (or on demand), so content stays fresh without rebuilding the whole site. In the App Router you set export const revalidate = N or fetch with next: { revalidate: N }.

title: What is on-demand revalidation in Next.js (revalidatePath / revalidateTag)?
description: Purging cached data/routes when content changes.
answer: revalidatePath(path) and revalidateTag(tag) invalidate cached routes/data immediately — typically called from a Server Action or webhook after content changes — so the next request regenerates fresh content instead of waiting for a time-based interval.

title: What is the Data Cache in Next.js and how do you opt out of it?
description: Persistent server-side cache for fetched data.
answer: The Data Cache stores fetch results on the server across requests and deployments to avoid refetching. Opt out per fetch with cache: 'no-store', per segment with export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' or revalidate = 0, so data is always fresh.

title: What is the Full Route Cache in Next.js?
description: Server-side cache of rendered static routes.
answer: The Full Route Cache stores the rendered HTML and RSC payload of statically-rendered routes at build time, so they're served instantly without re-rendering. It's invalidated by revalidation or a new deploy; dynamic routes skip it.

title: What is the Router Cache in Next.js and what are its implications?
description: Client-side cache of visited route segments.
answer: The Router Cache (client-side) stores the RSC payload of visited/prefetched routes for the session, giving instant back/forward navigation. Its staleness can show outdated data after a mutation, so you call router.refresh() or revalidate to update it.

title: How do you fetch data in Next.js Client Components (SWR, React Query, use)?
description: Client-side fetching when you need interactivity.
answer: In Client Components you fetch with libraries like SWR or React Query (caching, revalidation, mutations) or stream a Server Component promise into the use() hook. Prefer fetching in Server Components when possible; use client fetching for highly interactive/real-time data.

title: What is request memoization in Next.js?
description: Automatic deduping of identical fetches during one render.
answer: Within a single server render, Next.js memoizes identical fetch() calls (same URL/options) so multiple components requesting the same data trigger only one network call. This lets you fetch where you need data without prop-drilling or manual deduping.

title: How do you handle loading and error states for data fetching in Next.js?
description: loading.js / Suspense and error.js boundaries.
answer: Use loading.js (or <Suspense fallback>) to show loading UI while Server Component data resolves, and error.js to catch fetch/render failures with retry. For expected states (empty, not found) handle them in code, often with notFound().

title: What changed about caching defaults in Next.js 15?
description: Less aggressive caching out of the box.
answer: Next.js 15 made caching opt-in rather than aggressive-by-default: fetch and GET Route Handlers are no longer cached by default, and the client Router Cache no longer reuses page segments by default (staleTime 0). You opt into caching explicitly when you want it.

title: What are Server Actions in Next.js and how do they work?
description: Server-side functions callable from the client without an API route.
answer: Server Actions are async functions marked 'use server' that run on the server and can be called from components/forms directly — Next.js creates an RPC endpoint under the hood. They handle mutations (DB writes, revalidation) without you writing an API route.

title: How do you handle forms with Server Actions in Next.js (progressive enhancement)?
description: Wiring a form action to a server function.
answer: You pass a Server Action to a <form action={...}>; it receives FormData and runs on the server. Because it works without client JS, forms are progressively enhanced — they submit even before hydration — and you pair it with useActionState for pending/error UI.

title: How do you do optimistic updates in Next.js (useOptimistic, useActionState)?
description: Instant UI feedback while a Server Action runs.
answer: useOptimistic applies a temporary optimistic state immediately while the Server Action is pending, reverting if it fails. useActionState tracks the action's result and pending status. Together they give responsive forms/mutations with proper rollback.

title: How do you revalidate data after a mutation in Next.js?
description: Refreshing caches once a Server Action changes data.
answer: After a Server Action mutates data, call revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() to invalidate the affected caches, or router.refresh() on the client, so the UI reflects the change. Redirecting with redirect() also re-renders the target route fresh.

title: What are the security considerations for Server Actions in Next.js?
description: Treating actions as public endpoints.
answer: Server Actions are public HTTP endpoints, so you must authenticate/authorize inside each one, validate all inputs (zod), and never trust client-provided ids. Next.js adds protections (encrypted action ids, origin checks), but auth and validation are your responsibility.

title: How do Server Actions differ from Route Handlers in Next.js?
description: RPC-style mutations vs explicit HTTP endpoints.
answer: Server Actions are RPC-like functions co-located with components, ideal for form mutations and progressive enhancement. Route Handlers (route.js) are explicit REST/HTTP endpoints for webhooks, third-party clients, or public APIs. Use Actions for your own UI mutations, Handlers for external/HTTP needs.

title: What are Route Handlers in Next.js and how do they differ from Pages API routes?
description: app/.../route.js using Web Request/Response.
answer: Route Handlers (route.js in the App Router) define HTTP endpoints with named exports (GET, POST, …) using the Web Request/Response APIs. Unlike Pages API routes (req/res Node objects), they're Web-standard, run on Node or Edge, and live in the app/ tree.

title: How do you read request data and return responses in a Next.js Route Handler?
description: Web Request parsing and Response/NextResponse.
answer: A handler receives a standard Request: use await request.json()/formData(), request.nextUrl.searchParams, cookies(), and headers(). Return a Response or NextResponse (e.g. NextResponse.json(data, { status })). Dynamic params come from the second argument's params.

title: What is Middleware in Next.js and what can it do?
description: Edge code running before a request is completed.
answer: middleware.ts runs on every matching request before rendering, on the Edge runtime. Use it for auth gating, redirects/rewrites, A/B testing, geolocation, setting headers/cookies — but keep it lightweight (no DB, no heavy work) since it runs on the hot path.

title: What runtimes does Next.js support (Node.js vs Edge runtime)?
description: Full Node APIs vs lightweight global Edge.
answer: The Node.js runtime supports all Node APIs and is the default for most rendering/handlers. The Edge runtime is a lightweight, globally-distributed V8 environment with low cold starts and a limited API subset (no native Node modules) — ideal for Middleware and latency-sensitive handlers.

title: How does the Metadata API work in Next.js (static and generateMetadata)?
description: Declaring head tags from layouts/pages.
answer: Export a static metadata object or an async generateMetadata() function from a layout/page; Next.js renders the corresponding <title>, meta, Open Graph, and canonical tags. generateMetadata can fetch data (deduped with the page) to build dynamic, per-route SEO.

title: How do you implement dynamic Open Graph images in Next.js (ImageResponse)?
description: Generating social images at the edge with JSX.
answer: An opengraph-image.tsx file (or the ImageResponse API from next/og) renders JSX/CSS to a PNG on demand, so you generate per-page social share images with dynamic content (title, author) without a design tool or external service.

title: How do sitemap.ts and robots.ts work in Next.js?
description: Programmatic SEO files via convention.
answer: app/sitemap.ts exports a function returning your URLs (with lastModified, priority) and Next.js serves /sitemap.xml; app/robots.ts returns rules and serves /robots.txt. Both can be dynamic (fetch routes from a DB) and are generated at build or on request.

title: How does Next.js handle SEO compared to a client-rendered SPA?
description: Server-rendered HTML that crawlers see immediately.
answer: Because Next.js renders HTML on the server (SSR/SSG), crawlers get fully-formed content and metadata on first request — unlike a client-rendered SPA where bots may see an empty shell until JS runs. Plus the Metadata API, sitemaps, and fast loads boost SEO.

title: What is JSON-LD structured data and how do you add it in Next.js?
description: Embedding schema.org data for rich results.
answer: JSON-LD is schema.org structured data that helps search engines understand your content (articles, products, FAQs) and show rich results. In Next.js you render a <script type="application/ld+json"> with the JSON in a Server Component, often built from the same data as the page.

title: How does next/image optimize images in Next.js?
description: Automatic resizing, formats, and lazy loading.
answer: next/image serves correctly-sized, modern-format (WebP/AVIF) images on demand, lazy-loads by default, and reserves space (width/height) to prevent layout shift. It optimizes via a built-in loader/CDN, cutting bytes and improving Core Web Vitals.

title: How does next/font work and why does it improve performance?
description: Self-hosting fonts with zero layout shift.
answer: next/font downloads and self-hosts fonts (Google or local) at build time, removing external requests and using size-adjust/fallback metrics to eliminate layout shift (CLS). It also strips unused glyphs, improving load performance and privacy.

title: What is next/script and what are its strategy options in Next.js?
description: Controlling when third-party scripts load.
answer: next/script loads third-party scripts with a strategy: beforeInteractive (critical, before hydration), afterInteractive (default, after hydration), lazyOnload (idle), and worker (off main thread). It prevents blocking scripts from hurting performance.

title: How does code splitting and next/dynamic work in Next.js?
description: Lazy-loading components and route-based splitting.
answer: Next.js automatically code-splits per route. next/dynamic (or React.lazy) defers loading a heavy or client-only component until needed, with an optional loading fallback and ssr:false to skip server rendering — shrinking the initial bundle.

title: How does Next.js handle CSS (CSS Modules, global, Tailwind, CSS-in-JS)?
description: Styling options and their constraints with RSC.
answer: Next.js supports global CSS (imported in the root layout), CSS Modules (scoped per component), Tailwind, and Sass out of the box. Runtime CSS-in-JS libraries need a 'use client' boundary/registry since they don't run in Server Components; zero-runtime CSS works best with RSC.

title: What is the difference between the /public folder and imported assets in Next.js?
description: Static file serving vs build-pipeline assets.
answer: Files in /public are served as-is from the root URL (e.g. /logo.png) with no processing — good for robots.txt, favicons. Imported assets (import img from './a.png') go through the build pipeline, get hashed filenames and optimization, and enable next/image features.

title: How do you analyze and reduce bundle size in Next.js?
description: Bundle analyzer, RSC, and dynamic imports.
answer: Use @next/bundle-analyzer to visualize bundles, then shrink them: keep components as Server Components (zero client JS), lazy-load heavy client code with next/dynamic, avoid large dependencies/barrel imports, and move logic server-side. Check the build output's per-route JS sizes.

title: What is prefetching in Next.js and how does next/link prefetch routes?
description: Loading route code/data before navigation.
answer: next/link prefetches the linked route's JS and (for static routes) data when the link is visible/hovered, so navigation feels instant. Prefetch can be disabled with prefetch={false}; in dev it's off. It populates the client Router Cache ahead of time.

title: How do environment variables work in Next.js (the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix)?
description: Server-only vs browser-exposed env vars.
answer: Variables in .env are available on the server via process.env. Only those prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_ are inlined into the client bundle and exposed to the browser — so never prefix secrets. Next.js loads .env.local/.env.production etc. by convention.

title: What is the difference between build-time and runtime environment variables in Next.js?
description: Inlined-at-build vs read-at-request values.
answer: NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars are inlined at build time, so changing them requires a rebuild. Server-only vars read from process.env at runtime can change per environment without rebuilding (for dynamically-rendered code). This matters for Docker images promoted across environments.

title: How do you deploy a Next.js app (Vercel vs self-hosted)?
description: Managed platform vs Node server/standalone/container.
answer: Vercel offers zero-config hosting with built-in CDN, ISR, and edge functions. Self-hosting runs next start on a Node server or a Docker container (often output: 'standalone'); you then provide your own CDN, image optimizer, and revalidation infra. Static exports work for purely static sites.

title: What is the output: 'standalone' build in Next.js and when do you use it?
description: Minimal self-contained server bundle for containers.
answer: output: 'standalone' produces a minimal folder with the server and only the node_modules it needs, so Docker images are small and don't require installing all dependencies. Use it for self-hosted/containerized deployments to cut image size and cold starts.

title: How do you handle authentication in Next.js?
description: Sessions/JWT with middleware and Server Components.
answer: Authenticate (credentials/OAuth via NextAuth/Auth.js or your own), store a session in an HttpOnly cookie or JWT, gate routes in middleware for fast redirects, and verify the session in Server Components/Server Actions before data access. Never rely on client-only checks.

title: What is the difference between getServerSideProps/getStaticProps and App Router data fetching?
description: Pages data functions vs async Server Components.
answer: In the Pages Router, getStaticProps (build-time) and getServerSideProps (per-request) fetch data and pass props to a page. The App Router replaces both with async Server Components that fetch directly, using fetch caching/revalidate to choose static vs dynamic per call — more granular and colocated.

title: How do you migrate from the Pages Router to the App Router in Next.js?
description: Incremental, route-by-route adoption.
answer: app/ and pages/ can coexist, so you migrate incrementally: move routes into app/ one at a time, convert data functions to async Server Components, replace _app/_document with root layout.js, swap getServerSideProps/getStaticProps for fetch/segment config, and update next/router to next/navigation.

title: What is next.config.js in Next.js and what are common configurations?
description: The framework configuration file.
answer: next.config.js (or .ts/.mjs) configures the build and runtime: redirects(), rewrites(), headers(), images (remote patterns/loaders), output mode, experimental flags, env, transpilePackages, and webpack/turbopack tweaks. It's evaluated at build/start, not per request.

title: How do you handle redirects and rewrites in Next.js?
description: Config-based, middleware, and in-render redirects.
answer: Define static redirects()/rewrites() in next.config.js (redirect changes the URL; rewrite proxies internally while keeping the URL). For dynamic/conditional cases use middleware (NextResponse.redirect/rewrite) or call redirect() inside Server Components/Actions.

title: How do you internationalize (i18n) a Next.js App Router app?
description: Locale routing and translation in the App Router.
answer: The App Router has no built-in i18n routing (unlike Pages), so you use a [lang] dynamic segment plus middleware to detect/redirect by locale, and a library (next-intl, next-i18next) to load translations. generateStaticParams pre-renders each locale; metadata is localized per route.

title: What is Turbopack in Next.js and how does it compare to Webpack?
description: The Rust-based bundler for faster dev.
answer: Turbopack is Next.js's Rust-based bundler designed to replace Webpack, offering much faster cold starts and incremental updates (HMR) via fine-grained caching. It's the default for next dev in recent versions; Webpack remains the fallback while Turbopack's production builds mature.

title: How do you handle instrumentation and observability in Next.js (instrumentation.ts)?
description: Startup hooks for tracing/monitoring.
answer: instrumentation.ts exports a register() function that runs once when the server starts — the place to initialize OpenTelemetry, error monitoring (Sentry), or other tracing. Next.js also supports onRequestError and OpenTelemetry integration for distributed tracing across server rendering and handlers.
